SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper evaluates its geometric, per-pair subspace model on an appropriate adjective–noun dataset and compares to the original supervised and semi-supervised tensor baselines plus simple baselines, which supports the core performance claim. However, it lacks key ablations and sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying k, different vector spaces such as word2vec vs PMI, projection choices), does not report hyperparameters or release code, and offers no discussion of failure cases or limitations—weakening reproducibility and the zero-shot claim. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, but the experimental depth and reproducibility detail are insufficient to fully substantiate the methodological contributions. | B(3): The paper presents a novel geometric contextual approach and evaluates it on the Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective–noun dataset with reasonable baselines, so empirical results partially support the main claim of zero-shot identification. However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g. choice of k, projection strategy, logistic-regression features), omits hyperparameter reporting and code availability, and does not discuss failure cases or limitations. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, but reproducibility and deeper diagnostic evaluations necessary to substantiate claims about generalization to unseen metaphors are missing.

Related work coverage is reported as thorough, but the experimental depth and reproducibility detail are insufficient to fully substantiate the methodological contributions.

(2016) adjective–noun dataset with reasonable baselines, so empirical results partially support the main claim of zero-shot identification. However, it lacks ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g. choice of k, projection strategy, logistic-regression features), omits hyperparameter reporting and code availability, and does not discuss failure cases or limitations. Related work coverage is reported as thorough, but reproducibility and deeper diagnostic evaluations necessary to substantiate claims about generalization to unseen metaphors are missing.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NLP problem (metaphor identification) using distributional semantics and evaluation on a standard adjective-noun benchmark (Gutiérrez et al. 2016), which fits well within top-tier NLP/ML venues. The zero-shot, context-sensitive geometric approach and comparisons to established tensor baselines and simple cosine baselines target the right audience and evaluation norms. Minor concerns for a top venue include limited reporting of hyperparameters and lack of mentioned code/reproducibility artifacts, which are important to this community but do not fundamentally remove relevance. | B(4): This paper fits the scope and audience of a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a core NLP problem (metaphor identification) with a novel geometric, context-sensitive modeling idea and evaluates against established baselines (Gutiérrez et al. tensor methods, word2vec similarity) on a standard adjective–noun dataset. The problem formulation and evaluation (F-score on the 8,592 dyad dataset using PMI-derived vectors) match community expectations, but the submission omits practical details (hyperparameters, code availability) and does not state limitations, which are minor concerns for reproducibility and impact—hence a strong but not perfect fit.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (metaphor identification) using distributional semantics and evaluation on a standard adjective-noun benchmark (Gutiérrez et al. 2016), which fits well within top-tier NLP/ML venues. The zero-shot, context-sensitive geometric approach and comparisons to established tensor baselines and simple cosine baselines target the right audience and evaluation norms. Minor concerns for a top venue include limited reporting of hyperparameters and lack of mentioned code/reproducibility artifacts, which are important to this community but do not fundamentally remove relevance.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper fits the scope and audience of a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a core NLP problem (metaphor identification) with a novel geometric, context-sensitive modeling idea and evaluates against established baselines (Gutiérrez et al. tensor methods, word2vec similarity) on a standard adjective–noun dataset. The problem formulation and evaluation (F-score on the 8,592 dyad dataset using PMI-derived vectors) match community expectations, but the submission omits practical details (hyperparameters, code availability) and does not state limitations, which are minor concerns for reproducibility and impact—hence a strong but not perfect fit.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against the primary prior art for this dataset (Gutiérrez et al.'s supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods) and includes reasonable baselines (word2vec cosine and majority), using the same Gutiérrez et al. adjective‑noun dataset and F‑score metric, which supports fair head‑to‑head comparison. However, important methodological details that affect fairness are missing (no hyperparameters reported, no code availability, and no significance testing), and the work omits evaluation against other contemporary representation or zero‑shot metaphor approaches that would strengthen claims of novelty. The related work coverage is described as thorough, and the paper does acknowledge comparable performance to the supervised tensor baseline, but the empirical comparisons would be more convincing with fuller ablations and reproducibility details. | B(4): The paper evaluates against the main prior benchmarks for adjective–noun metaphor detection (Gutiérrez et al. 2016 supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods) and includes reasonable baselines (word2vec cosine + logistic regression and majority). Comparisons appear fair given the use of the same Gutiérrez dataset and F‑score metric, and the paper acknowledges when prior supervised methods achieve comparable performance. However, the work seems to omit other contemporary metaphor/metaphoricity approaches beyond the Gutiérrez family (and does not report hyperparameter/code details), so while comparisons are strong they are not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against the primary prior art for this dataset (Gutiérrez et al.'s supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods) and includes reasonable baselines (word2vec cosine and majority), using the same Gutiérrez et al. adjective‑noun dataset and F‑score metric, which supports fair head‑to‑head comparison. However, important methodological details that affect fairness are missing (no hyperparameters reported, no code availability, and no significance testing), and the work omits evaluation against other contemporary representation or zero‑shot metaphor approaches that would strengthen claims of novelty. The related work coverage is described as thorough, and the paper does acknowledge comparable performance to the supervised tensor baseline, but the empirical comparisons would be more convincing with fuller ablations and reproducibility details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against the main prior benchmarks for adjective–noun metaphor detection (Gutiérrez et al. 2016 supervised and semi‑supervised tensor methods) and includes reasonable baselines (word2vec cosine + logistic regression and majority). Comparisons appear fair given the use of the same Gutiérrez dataset and F‑score metric, and the paper acknowledges when prior supervised methods achieve comparable performance. However, the work seems to omit other contemporary metaphor/metaphoricity approaches beyond the Gutiérrez family (and does not report hyperparameter/code details), so while comparisons are strong they are not fully comprehensive.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(3): The paper presents a plausible geometric per-pair subspace method and provides experimental comparisons on the Gutiérrez et al. adjective–noun dataset showing F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor baseline and better than a semi-supervised one, so the central empirical claim is partially supported. However, important methodological details are missing (no hyperparameter/settings reported, no code, no statistical significance testing) and several critical ablations/sensitivity analyses are absent (varying k, alternative projection methods, different vector spaces/corpora, runtime cost), which weakens confidence in the zero-shot and robustness claims. The mathematical framing appears reasonable from the summary, but without proofs or fuller derivations and with unreported implementation choices there are logical gaps about how stable and general the approach is. Given these omissions the results are promising but require additional experimental rigor to fully justify the conclusions. | B(4): The paper states clear empirical claims and supports them with experiments on the Gutiérrez et al. (2016) AN dataset showing comparable F-scores to a supervised tensor baseline and improvement over their semi-supervised variant, which provides substantive evidence for the method. However, the summary indicates missing details that affect reproducibility and technical completeness: no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance testing, no code release, and no mathematical proofs for the proposed geometric selection procedure — these are minor but important gaps. Overall the methodology appears sound (reasonable baselines and a large PMI-based vector source), but the lack of reported hyperparameters and significance analyses prevents me from assigning a top score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a plausible geometric per-pair subspace method and provides experimental comparisons on the Gutiérrez et al. adjective–noun dataset showing F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor baseline and better than a semi-supervised one, so the central empirical claim is partially supported. However, important methodological details are missing (no hyperparameter/settings reported, no code, no statistical significance testing) and several critical ablations/sensitivity analyses are absent (varying k, alternative projection methods, different vector spaces/corpora, runtime cost), which weakens confidence in the zero-shot and robustness claims. The mathematical framing appears reasonable from the summary, but without proofs or fuller derivations and with unreported implementation choices there are logical gaps about how stable and general the approach is. Given these omissions the results are promising but require additional experimental rigor to fully justify the conclusions.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and supports them with experiments on the Gutiérrez et al. (2016) AN dataset showing comparable F-scores to a supervised tensor baseline and improvement over their semi-supervised variant, which provides substantive evidence for the method. However, the summary indicates missing details that affect reproducibility and technical completeness: no hyperparameter reporting, no mention of statistical significance testing, no code release, and no mathematical proofs for the proposed geometric selection procedure — these are minor but important gaps. Overall the methodology appears sound (reasonable baselines and a large PMI-based vector source), but the lack of reported hyperparameters and significance analyses prevents me from assigning a top score.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early; the introduction, method, experiments, and conclusion follow a clear logical flow and related work coverage is thorough. Notation and presentation are generally consistent and the writing quality is good, with effective figures/tables/examples supporting the method. However, important reproducibility-related presentation gaps remain: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and the paper omits discussion of limitations—these omissions reduce clarity for readers wanting to reproduce or extend the work. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from motivation to method and experiments, and the main contributions (geometric context model, zero-shot capability, competitive results on the Gutiérrez et al. dataset) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the methodology (constructing per-pair subspaces from PMI-based vectors and classifying with logistic regression) is communicated clearly; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the related work coverage is thorough. Minor presentation gaps reduce clarity: hyperparameter choices are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and no limitations are discussed, which would help reproducibility and situate claims about ‘zero-shot’ behavior more precisely.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early; the introduction, method, experiments, and conclusion follow a clear logical flow and related work coverage is thorough. Notation and presentation are generally consistent and the writing quality is good, with effective figures/tables/examples supporting the method. However, important reproducibility-related presentation gaps remain: hyperparameters are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and the paper omits discussion of limitations—these omissions reduce clarity for readers wanting to reproduce or extend the work.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear logical flow from motivation to method and experiments, and the main contributions (geometric context model, zero-shot capability, competitive results on the Gutiérrez et al. dataset) are stated early. Notation appears consistent and the methodology (constructing per-pair subspaces from PMI-based vectors and classifying with logistic regression) is communicated clearly; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and the related work coverage is thorough. Minor presentation gaps reduce clarity: hyperparameter choices are not reported, code availability is not mentioned, and no limitations are discussed, which would help reproducibility and situate claims about ‘zero-shot’ behavior more precisely.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper proposes a notable and well-differentiated idea: constructing ad-hoc k-dimensional geometric subspaces per adjective-noun pair to detect metaphors, which is a fresh perspective relative to global tensor-based supervised models and enables an effective zero-shot capability. This per-instance, context-sensitive projection using only individual word vectors appears to be a non-trivial methodological shift from prior supervised/semi-supervised tensor approaches (Gutiérrez et al.), so it would likely inform experts; however, it builds on established distributional-vector foundations and is not a radical departure from existing representation-learning techniques. | B(4): The paper proposes a clearly new geometric, context-sensitive formulation—constructing ad-hoc k-dimensional subspaces per adjective-noun pair and classifying metaphoricity from projections—that departs from prior tensor and similarity-based approaches. Framing metaphor identification as bespoke subspace induction using only individual word vectors and operating effectively zero-shot is a notable methodological novelty beyond standard supervised tensor learning or cosine-based baselines. The idea is a non-trivial technical extension with clear differentiation from Gutiérrez et al. (2016) methods, and an expert would find the geometric, per-pair subspace perspective informative even though metaphor identification itself is a well-studied problem.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper proposes a notable and well-differentiated idea: constructing ad-hoc k-dimensional geometric subspaces per adjective-noun pair to detect metaphors, which is a fresh perspective relative to global tensor-based supervised models and enables an effective zero-shot capability. This per-instance, context-sensitive projection using only individual word vectors appears to be a non-trivial methodological shift from prior supervised/semi-supervised tensor approaches (Gutiérrez et al.), so it would likely inform experts; however, it builds on established distributional-vector foundations and is not a radical departure from existing representation-learning techniques.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper proposes a clearly new geometric, context-sensitive formulation—constructing ad-hoc k-dimensional subspaces per adjective-noun pair and classifying metaphoricity from projections—that departs from prior tensor and similarity-based approaches. Framing metaphor identification as bespoke subspace induction using only individual word vectors and operating effectively zero-shot is a notable methodological novelty beyond standard supervised tensor learning or cosine-based baselines. The idea is a non-trivial technical extension with clear differentiation from Gutiérrez et al. (2016) methods, and an expert would find the geometric, per-pair subspace perspective informative even though metaphor identification itself is a well-studied problem.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Identifying unseen metaphors is an important problem in semantics and NLP, and the paper's zero-shot, per-pair geometric subspace approach—using only pretrained word vectors and achieving F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor method on the 8,592-instance Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective-noun dataset—could meaningfully shift how researchers model context-sensitive compositionality. The method’s ability to operate without phrase representations or supervised training suggests a broadly applicable paradigm that could motivate follow-up work on contextual subspaces and resource-light metaphor detection. Given the empirical claim of parity with a supervised baseline and the reliance on standard corpora (Wikipedia PMI vectors), the contribution is likely to be adopted and extended, though its ultimate influence will depend on reproducibility details and robustness across other corpora and embedding choices. | B(4): This paper tackles the important problem of identifying novel metaphors, and proposes a linguistically-plausible, context-sensitive geometric method that constructs bespoke subspaces per adjective–noun pair and operates in a zero-shot fashion using only pretrained word vectors (PMI-based). The method achieves F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor approach on the substantial Gutiérrez et al. (2016) AN dataset, suggesting a viable alternative to heavy supervision and phrase-level representations. By reframing metaphoricity as a local subspace phenomenon, the work could change how researchers model context-dependent meaning and open new directions for lightweight, generalizable metaphor detection. Adoption may be somewhat limited until code, hyperparameters, and broader evaluations (other languages/parts of speech) are provided, but the conceptual contribution is significant.

reviewer_a.justification: Identifying unseen metaphors is an important problem in semantics and NLP, and the paper's zero-shot, per-pair geometric subspace approach—using only pretrained word vectors and achieving F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor method on the 8,592-instance Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective-noun dataset—could meaningfully shift how researchers model context-sensitive compositionality. The method’s ability to operate without phrase representations or supervised training suggests a broadly applicable paradigm that could motivate follow-up work on contextual subspaces and resource-light metaphor detection. Given the empirical claim of parity with a supervised baseline and the reliance on standard corpora (Wikipedia PMI vectors), the contribution is likely to be adopted and extended, though its ultimate influence will depend on reproducibility details and robustness across other corpora and embedding choices.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles the important problem of identifying novel metaphors, and proposes a linguistically-plausible, context-sensitive geometric method that constructs bespoke subspaces per adjective–noun pair and operates in a zero-shot fashion using only pretrained word vectors (PMI-based). The method achieves F-scores comparable to a supervised tensor approach on the substantial Gutiérrez et al. (2016) AN dataset, suggesting a viable alternative to heavy supervision and phrase-level representations. By reframing metaphoricity as a local subspace phenomenon, the work could change how researchers model context-dependent meaning and open new directions for lightweight, generalizable metaphor detection. Adoption may be somewhat limited until code, hyperparameters, and broader evaluations (other languages/parts of speech) are provided, but the conceptual contribution is significant.

main_claims: Introduces a geometric, context-sensitive model that generates a bespoke subspace for each candidate adjective-noun pair to identify metaphors.
The model requires only individual word vectors (not phrase representations) and is effectively zero-shot, able to identify novel/unseen metaphors.
On the Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective-noun dataset, the model achieves F-scores comparable to the supervised tensor method of Gutiérrez et al. and superior to their semi-supervised method.
_summary.methodology: Given pretrained distributional word vectors (PMI-based from a large corpus), the method selects k-dimensional subspaces per candidate word pair based on independent and correlated properties of the two word vectors, projects the pair into this ad-hoc geometric space, and classifies metaphoricity using logistic regression on features of the subspace.
datasets: Gutiérrez et al. (2016) adjective-noun dataset (8,592 adjective-noun dyads)
December 2014 English Wikipedia dump (used to compute PMI co-occurrence statistics)
baselines: Gutiérrez et al. (2016) supervised tensor method
Gutiérrez et al. (2016) semi-supervised tensor method (their V2)

reviewer_a.name: Methodological ML Engineer
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with a PhD in computational linguistics who has led reproducible NLP experiments and deployed vector-space models in production. Extensive experience designing ablations, baselines, and evaluation suites for representation-learning papers.
traits: checks for proper baselines, statistical significance, and whether comparisons are fair (same data/splits/hyperparameters)
demands detailed ablation studies and sensitivity analyses (e.g., varying k, choice of vector space, and projection method)
looks for reproducibility: clear hyperparameters, training details, and availability of code/data
evaluates robustness to corpus and embedding choices and runtime/complexity trade-offs
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on experimental rigor and implementation clarity, highlighting missing controls or analysis needed to support empirical claims.

reviewer_b.name: Semantic NLP Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor in NLP and cognitive semantics, with a track record of work on metaphor, figurative language, and lexical semantics; familiar with distributional approaches and linguistically motivated evaluation benchmarks.
traits: assesses novelty relative to prior metaphor-identification and context-aware embedding work and situates contributions within the literature
values linguistic plausibility and analysis of error types and interpretability of model behavior
considers broader impact: applicability to downstream tasks, linguistic generalization (languages/parts of speech), and theoretical implications
notices whether claims about 'zero-shot' and 'unseen' are linguistically and empirically justified
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Evaluates conceptual novelty and real-world significance, asking for linguistic analysis and clearer articulation of how the work advances the field.
